models_tested.notes: evals 0-2 dated 2026-03-14 (initial narrated/simulated runs); evals 3-4 backfilled 2026-04-22 with live-workspace grading — this entry's `timestamp` reflects the most recent Sonnet run

notes: Multi-model benchmark: all 5 evals ran on both claude-sonnet-4-6 and claude-opus-4-7 (1 run per eval per configuration; evals 0-2 on Sonnet dated 2026-03-14, evals 3-4 on Sonnet backfilled 2026-04-22; all Opus runs dated 2026-04-22). Per-model stats live in run_summary_by_model; the top-level run_summary mirrors the latest model (claude-opus-4-7) for compatibility with the single-model aggregate_benchmark.py script.

On claude-sonnet-4-6 the skill produces +8% pass-rate lift across 5 evals (was +13% with 3 evals; +10% with 4 evals — diluted each time a non-discriminating eval was added). The lift is still concentrated entirely in eval 2, where the baseline scored 3/5 vs. with-skill 5/5 on multi-target routing and plan-before-apply. On claude-opus-4-7 the baseline reaches all 27 assertions per configuration (5+5+5+6+6 across evals 0-4) on its own — lift is +0% across all five evals.

Evals 3 and 4 were targeted discrimination attempts for Opus-tier baselines. Eval 3 tests Route A's 'search existing content before appending' rule (update-in-place vs. duplicate). Eval 4 tests Route A's scope-guard behavior when multiple AI configs are present (asks which to update vs. silent broadcast). Neither discriminated on either model: both Sonnet and Opus baselines update in place correctly and ask disambiguation questions unprompted when multiple configs are detected. On Opus 4.7 the baseline prompt in eval 4 was even more nuanced than the skill's — it noticed the Copilot file was style-scoped and recommended skipping it.

Self-graded by executor for all runs — no separate analyzer pass. Earlier Sonnet 4.6 runs on evals 0-2 used the original narrated/simulated grading methodology; evals 3-4 Sonnet runs used the same live-workspace self-grading as Opus.
